content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 6,
1993
Gloria S.
McLean
Office of the City Attorney
City of Santee
10765 Woodside Avenue
Santee,
CA
92071—3198
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-339
Dear Ms.
McLean:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Santee City Councilmeinbers Hal Ryan, Edith French and James Romine
regarding their duties under the conflict—of—interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act.
1
! This letter will also serve to
confirm telephone advice which you received from this office on
September 8,
1993.
QUESTION
Are Councilmembers Hal Ryan, Edith French and James Romine
prohibited from voting on a rent control ordinance which would
impose rental ceilings on mobile home park spaces?
CONCLUSION
Councilmembers Ryan,
French and Romine do not have economic
interests which would be affected by the rent control ordinance.
2
!
Therefore, they may vote on the ordinance.
1!
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Coimuission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000
q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2!
This advice only applies to the economic interests identified
in this letter.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
A-93-339
Page Two
FACTS
The Santee City Council will be voting on a proposed
ordinance which would impose rental ceilings on mobile home park
spaces.
One of the mobile home parks which would be affected the
ordinance is the Valley View Mobile Home Park.
This park is owned
by Richard and Jean McManus.
Councilmember Ryan
Councilmember Ryan is a real estate broker with Century 21,
and held a listing agreement with a client for the sale of
residential real property.
Mr.
Ryan received an offer on the
listing from Richard and Jean McManus accompanied by a $500 non-
refundable check.
Mr.
Ryan noted that the offering persons were
park owners.
With the consent of his client, he marked “void” on
the check and transferred his listing to another broker with whom
he has no financial connection.
He then advised Mr.
and Mrs.
McManus to deal with the unrelated broker in negotiating the
purchase.
Councilmember French
Councilmember French is the majority owner of Eagle Fence
Company.
Ms.
French’s company contracted with a customer to
construct a fence for the price of $672.
The fence was
constructed in accordance with the contract.
Couricilmember French
received a check in the amount of $672 from Jean McManus on behalf
of the customer as payment for the fence construction.
Counciimember Romine
Councilmember Romine works on commission as a dealer for
various novelty products.
He sells products on behalf of a
company known as H.W.
Singleton Company,
Inc.
A customer
identified himself as
“Mr.
Walker” and ordered 100 T—shirts to be
imprinted with a custom statement.
A down payment of $260 in cash
was given to Mr.
Romine.
That cash was converted to a money order
and sent to the H.W.
Singleton Company,
Inc.
Mr. Walker attempted
to pay the balance owed,
$284, with a check signed by Mr.
McManus.
You indicated to me in a telephone conversation that Councilmember
Romine refused to accept the check from Mr. Walker.
As a
commissioned salesman,
Councilmember Romine’s commission on the
transaction was less than $50.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.


Advice File No.
A—93-339
Page Three
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official’s immediate
family, or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
***
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10—percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103(a). and
(c)
Accordingly,
a public official may not make, participate in
making,
or attempt to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate family, or on
any of the official’s economic interests specified in Section
87103 above.


Advice File No.
A-93-339
Page Four
The term “investment” as used above means any financial
interest in or security issued by a business entity,
including but
not limited to common stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants,
options,
debt instruments and any partnership or other ownership
interest owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public
official,
or his or her immediate family.
(Section 82034.)
The term “income” as used above means,
a payment received,
including but not limited to any salary,
wage,
advance, dividend,
interest, rent, proceeds from any sale,
or gift.
Income of an
individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns
directly, indirectly or beneficially,
a 10-percent interest or
greater.
(Section 82030.)
Accordingly,
Councilmembers Ryan,
French and Romine may not
make, participate in making,
or attempt to use their official
position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable
3
! material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on their
investment interests, sources of income or businesses in which
they hold a business position.
Councilmember Ryan’s Economic Interests
Councilmember Ryan is a real estate broker.
He has a
financial interest in his brokerage business,
Century 21.
(Section 87103(a)
and (3).)
In addition,
he has a pro rata share
of any income to the business entity.
(Section 87103(c).)
In real estate transactions, the sources of income to a real
estate broker are:
(A)
The person the broker represents in the
transaction;
(B)
If the broker receives a commission from a
transaction conducted by an agent working under the
broker’s auspices, the person represented by the agent;
(C)
Any brokerage business entity through which
the broker conducts business;
and
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(ji
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Advice File No.
A—93—339
Page Five
(0)
Any person who receives a finder’s fee or
other referral fee for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker,
or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.
(Regulation 18704.3(c) (2).)
Accordingly, the parties listed above would be sources of
promised
4
! income to Councilmember Ryan and he would be prohibited
from participating in decisions affecting those sources if his
commission income amounted to more than $250.
Councilmember Ryan represented the seller in this
transaction.
Thus, his sources of income would have been the
seller, the agent working under his auspices,
and any person
receiving a finder’s fee.
Mr.
and Mrs.
McManus, the purchasers,
did not become a source of income to Councilmember Ryan by virtue
of the deposit and offer on the property.
(Romney Advice Letter,
A—88—063.
5/
Accordingly,
Councilmember Ryan is not prohibited from
participating in the vote on the proposed ordinance.
Councilmember French’s Economic Interests
Councilmember French is the majority owner of Eagle Fence
Company.
She has a financial interest in her fencing business,
Eagle Fence,
and has a pro rata share of any income tO the
business entity.
(Sections 87103 (a) (c)
and (e).)
Eagle Fence Company entered into a contract with a customer
to construct a fence for $672.
Upon completion of the fence, the
customer had incurred a debt to Eagle Fence Company in the amount
of $672.
When Eagle Fence Company received its payment, it was
from Jean McManus on behalf of the customer.
The customer had the legal obligation to pay Eagle Fence for
the services it rendered.
The fact that the payment was made by a
third party does not alter that conclusion.
We previously advised
that the patient to an optometrist, rather than the patient’s
insurance carrier,
was the source of income to the optometrist.
In real estate transactions,
income is deemed “promised” once
a sale is pending, i.e., the property is in escrow.
(Berqman
Advice Letter A-93-297.)
This analysis differs from that provided to you on the
telephone.
After further review of the regulation governing the
source of income in real estate transactions, it is concluded that
Mr.
and Mrs.
McManus would not have been the source of income to
Councilmember Ryan.


Advice File No.
A-93-339
Page Six
(Morris Advice Letter,
No.
1-90-373.)
Further,
when a
councilmember received a monetary judgment against a party in a
lawsuit and payment was made by an insurance carrier on behalf of
a party to a lawsuit, the party, not the insurance company, was
the source of income.
(See Barbosa Advice Letter No.
1—91—365 and
Olson Advice Letter No.
1-93—259.)
Similarly, if payment had been made by the customer’s
homeowner’s insurance policy as a result of weather damage, the
insurance company would merely be acting as the agent for the
customer.
Accordingly, Jean McManus was not a source of income to
Councilmember French when making a payment on behalf of the
customer.
Councilmember French does not have an economic interest
in Richard and Jean McManus.
He is therefore, not prohibited from
participating in the vote on the proposed ordinance.
Councilmember Romine’s Economic Interests
Councilinember Romine is a dealer for various novelty products
working on a commission basis.
As a commissioned salesman, his
sources of income are:
(A)
The person, store or other business entity
which provides the salesperson with the product or
service to sell and for which the salesperson acts as a
representative in the transaction;
and
-
(B)
The person who purchases the product or
service.
H.W.
Singleton Company, the company providing the T-shirts,
as well as Mr. Walker, the customer, are sources of commission
income to Councilmember Romine.
The full gross value of the
commission income for a specific sale is attributed to each source
of income in the transaction.
(Regulation 18704.3(d).)
Mr.
Walker attempted to pay the balance owed on the T-shirts
with a check from Richard McManus.
Councilmember Romine refused
to accept the check.
We have previously advised that a public
official receives no income for the purposes of the Act when the
official refuses to accept compensation
(Albano Advice Letter,
A—92—l91..)
Additionally, the commission income which Councilmember
Romine did receive from the transaction was less than $50 and does
not meet the monetary threshold to be an economic interest under
Section 87103(c).


Advice File No.
A-93—339
Page Seven
Accordingly,
Couricilmember Romine is not prohibited from
participating in the vote on the proposed ordinance.
I trust this has answered your question sufficiently.
If
there is anything further you need, please feel free to contact
this office at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SC/JET!jt

prohibited from voting on a rent control ordinance which would
impose rental ceilings on mobile home park spaces?

in this letter.
 •

Advice Page Two
